news_summary: Today’s environmental progress highlights a significant shift toward renewable energy in the U.S., inspiring youth-led ecological activism in the Dominican Republic, innovative efforts to protect coral reefs, and new scientific insights into Earth's resilience after catastrophic events.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées environnementales actuelles témoignent d'une transition majeure vers les énergies renouvelables aux États-Unis, suscitent un activisme écologique mené par les jeunes en République dominicaine, donnent lieu à des initiatives innovantes pour la protection des récifs coralliens et apportent de nouvelles connaissances scientifiques sur la résilience de la Terre après des catastrophes.
news_summary_es: Los avances medioambientales actuales ponen de relieve un cambio significativo hacia las energías renovables en Estados Unidos, lo que está inspirando el activismo ecológico impulsado por los jóvenes en la República Dominicana, iniciativas innovadoras para proteger los arrecifes de coral y nuevos conocimientos científicos sobre la resiliencia de la Tierra tras acontecimientos catastróficos.

articles.title: For first time, Americans are getting more of their electricity from solar than coal
articles.content:
Solar energy just provided more electricity in the United States than coal for the first time on record — marking a milestone for the rise of renewables in America.

While gas and nuclear plants still lead the country’s energy mix, solar contributed 12.8 percent of the nation’s electrons in May, according to an analysis of government data by Ember, an energy think tank. Coal, meanwhile, provided just 12.2 percent. Just five years ago, solar was less than half of its current levels and coal was at 20 percent.

“Overtaking coal for the first month on record shows just how far solar has come, from a niche contributor to the third-largest and fastest-growing source of power in the U.S. electricity system,” said Nicolas Fulghum, senior data analyst at Ember, in a press release. “From Texas to California, markets across the U.S. are betting on solar to meet rising power needs.”

The turnaround comes even as political headwinds have shifted against renewable energy.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Last summer, Congress passed the “One Big Beautiful Bill Act,” which rolled back enormous swaths of former President Joe Biden’s landmark climate change legislation, the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act. And President Donald Trump has actively sought to hinder renewable energy development, even offering to pay at least one oil company $1 billion to stop building its offshore wind projects.

The latest electricity data comes the same month that the Trump administration announced $700 million in funding for investments in the coal industry. It included money for what would be the country’s first new coal-fired power plants in 13 years — sourced from funds previously dedicated to reducing the country’s dependence on fossil fuels, not deepening it.

“Today we’re taking historic action to bring down the price of energy and the cost of living for all Americans with the power of clean, beautiful coal,” said Trump, who campaigned on the coal-friendly slogan ‘dig, baby, dig.”

Ember’s analysis found that coal generation in May was actually up slightly from April, when it hit an all-time low. Its share of the grid will also likely tick up in the summer, as cooling needs peak. But the steady downward trend over the last several years suggests that even all the president’s men might not be able to put the coal industry back together again.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

“Spending $700 million to bail out the coal industry is like throwing a lifeline to a ship that has already sunk,” Lena Moffitt, executive director of the environmental group Evergreen Action, told the Associated Press. Rich Nolan, president and CEO of the National Mining Association disagreed, telling the AP that coal generation helps shield consumers from the impacts of volatile energy prices and supply challenges exacerbated by AI.

Regardless of what coal does, experts believe the solar market will continue its upward march. While installations dropped in 2025 compared to 2024, according to the Solar Energy Industry Association, it still accounted for more than half of all newly installed electricity capacity. Even MAGA influencers are promoting it.

“We’re going to just keep seeing more and more renewables brought onto the grid,” said Patrick Drupp, director of climate policy at the Sierra Club. “That’s good for people’s wallets, it’s good for their health, it’s good for the planet.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant milestone where solar energy has surpassed coal in electricity generation in the U.S. for the first time, indicating meaningful progress in renewable energy adoption with broad positive environmental and societal impact. It provides detailed context about the energy mix, political challenges, and future outlook, focusing on a single important topic.
articles.personality_title: Solar energy passes coal in U.S. electricity for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the United States, electricity comes from different sources like coal, natural gas, nuclear power, and renewables such as solar energy. For many years, coal was one of the main sources of electricity, but solar power has been growing quickly.

**What happened** – In May, solar energy provided 12.8% of the electricity in the U.S., while coal provided 12.2%. This is the first time on record that solar has generated more electricity than coal. Five years ago, solar produced less than half as much electricity, and coal made up about 20% of the total.

**Impact** – This change shows how solar power is becoming a major player in the U.S. energy system. Despite political challenges and efforts to support coal, solar energy is growing faster and is now the third-largest source of electricity. This shift means cleaner energy is becoming more common, which can reduce pollution and help the environment.

**What’s next step** – Experts expect solar energy to keep growing, adding more capacity to the electric grid. Even though coal might increase slightly during summer, the long-term trend favors solar. This could lead to more investments in renewable energy and less reliance on coal and other fossil fuels.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, solar power has overtaken coal in the U.S. electricity mix, marking a key step forward for renewable energy.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'énergie solaire dépasse le charbon dans la production d'électricité américaine pour la première fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aux États-Unis, l'électricité provient de différentes sources comme le charbon, le gaz naturel, le nucléaire et les énergies renouvelables telles que l'énergie solaire. Pendant longtemps, le charbon était une source principale, mais l'énergie solaire se développe rapidement.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En mai, l'énergie solaire a fourni 12,8 % de l'électricité aux États-Unis, tandis que le charbon en fournissait 12,2 %. C'est la première fois qu'on observe que l'énergie solaire produit plus d'électricité que le charbon. Il y a cinq ans, le solaire produisait moins de la moitié de cette quantité, et le charbon représentait environ 20 %.

**Impact** – Ce changement montre que l'énergie solaire devient un acteur majeur du système électrique américain. Malgré des obstacles politiques et des efforts pour soutenir le charbon, le solaire croît plus vite et est désormais la troisième source d'électricité. Cela signifie une énergie plus propre, ce qui peut réduire la pollution et protéger l'environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts s'attendent à ce que l'énergie solaire continue de croître, en ajoutant plus de capacité au réseau électrique. Même si le charbon peut augmenter un peu en été, la tendance à long terme favorise le solaire. Cela pourrait entraîner plus d'investissements dans les énergies renouvelables et moins de dépendance au charbon.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, l'énergie solaire a dépassé le charbon dans le mix électrique américain, marquant une étape importante pour les énergies renouvelables.
articles.personality_title_es: La energía solar supera al carbón en la electricidad de EE. UU. por primera vez
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Estados Unidos, la electricidad proviene de diferentes fuentes como el carbón, el gas natural, la energía nuclear y las renovables como la energía solar. Durante muchos años, el carbón fue una de las principales fuentes, pero la energía solar ha estado creciendo rápidamente.

**Qué pasó** – En mayo, la energía solar proporcionó el 12.8 % de la electricidad en EE. UU., mientras que el carbón aportó el 12.2 %. Esta es la primera vez registrada en que la energía solar genera más electricidad que el carbón. Hace cinco años, la solar producía menos de la mitad y el carbón representaba alrededor del 20 %.

**Impacto** – Este cambio muestra que la energía solar se está convirtiendo en un actor importante en el sistema eléctrico estadounidense. A pesar de los desafíos políticos y los esfuerzos para apoyar al carbón, la solar crece más rápido y ahora es la tercera fuente más grande de electricidad. Esto significa energía más limpia, lo que puede reducir la contaminación y ayudar al medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos esperan que la energía solar siga creciendo, agregando más capacidad a la red eléctrica. Aunque el carbón podría aumentar un poco en verano, la tendencia a largo plazo favorece a la solar. Esto podría llevar a más inversiones en energías renovables y menos dependencia del carbón.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, la energía solar ha superado al carbón en la mezcla eléctrica de EE. UU., marcando un paso clave para las energías renovables.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant landscape painting featuring a large, radiant solar panel field glowing warmly under a bright sky, gently overshadowing a smaller, fading coal mine with dark smoke wisps dissipating into the air, symbolizing the transition from coal to solar energy in the United States.

articles.title: Meet three young people driving ecological activism in the Dominican Republic
articles.summary: The DR’s tourism star is on the rise, but that narrative of prosperity conceals the less visible, more urgent issue of environmental pressure on coasts, rivers, and reefs.
articles.content:
Environmental activism efforts in the Dominican Republic. Photo via Raíz Climáctica, used with permission.

This article by Zahiris Priscila Francisco Martínez first appeared in Raíz Climáctica on February 14, 2026. An edited version is being republished on Global Voices under a content partnership agreement.

This story is part of Global Voices’ May 2026 Spotlight, “Global crisis, local solutions.” This series offers stories of resistance and successful climate action, insight into how communities in the Global South are fighting back against the crisis, analysis of what this might mean for future generations, and more. You can support this coverage by donating here.

The Dominican Republic closed 2025 with an historic record of 11,676,901 visitors, a figure announced by authorities and confirmed in official reports that say it reflects sustained growth in the tourism sector, as well as increased air and maritime connectivity. The country’s participation in the International Tourism Fair (FITUR) in January 2026 echoed these claims: negotiations and agreements worth more than USD 13.37 billion are expected to expand the hotel supply with nearly 10,000 new rooms in the coming years, and, according to consolidated reports from the DR’s tourism intelligence system, the national average hotel occupancy rate in 2025 exceeded 71 percent.

That narrative of prosperity, however, conceals the less visible but more urgent issue of environmental pressure on coasts, rivers, and reefs. With more than 1,600 kilometers (994 miles) of coastline, including beaches like Punta Cana, Bávaro, Saona Island, Bayahibe, and Bahía de las Águilas, the relationship between growth and waste has become tangible in ways that some experts had already predicted. A 2023 market study led by Intellectual Capital Exchange (ICEX) on urban solid waste, reported that the country generates more than seven million tons of solid waste per year, with an average per capita generation of 650 kilograms per inhabitant. Such indicators place waste management as a bottleneck for tourism sustainability.

Management infrastructure has not grown at the pace of demand. Diagnoses by the government and international cooperation agencies documented the existence of around 240 open-air dumps. While some of these have since been closed, in 2021 the Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources, with support from the Japan International Cooperation Agency (JICA), reported a connection between the dumps and the leakage of waste into watersheds and coastlines. In addition, baseline diagnoses on marine pollution identified that the municipality of Santo Domingo Este generates about 72,738 tons of plastic waste annually; of this, thousands of tons that are not collected end up in bodies of water. Such volumes explain why beaches and mangroves constantly receive debris that degrades habitats.

Legislatively, there has been progress: the General Law on Comprehensive Waste Management and Co-processing (Number 225–20), enacted in 2020 with further amendments in 2023–2025, established a framework to distribute responsibilities among producers, municipalities, and the state. It also incorporated economic instruments such as incentives and green bonds to promote circularity. However, implementation and municipal financing have lagged behind; municipalities continue operating under budget limitations that hinder investment in technology and modernisation of collection services, revealing the gap between regulation and operational reality.

Against this backdrop, youth ocean activism has experienced constant tension: on one hand, the joy of seeing more tourists discover the beaches; on the other, the fatigue caused by the knowledge that management measures and infrastructure are not keeping pace with growth. This ambivalence between tourism expansion and structural limitations in waste management fuels both the technical agenda and the emotional burden carried by those protecting the seas, as sustainability simultaneously requires effective policies, investment, and care for the people sustaining the cause.

Ismael Sánchez: A historic environmental victory

Ismael Sánchez, originally from Santo Domingo, nurtures a deep affection for the sea and is determined to transform that passion into concrete change. His motivation comes from spending hours on the coast, seeing the life sustained by reefs, and feeling an intimate responsibility toward it. “The ocean gives us oxygen, regulates the climate, sustains millions of lives and economies,” he emphasised. “If we lose it, we lose ourselves.”

This conviction has pushed him to act, first through community cleanups and later through more technical and organised initiatives. His interest in activism was awakened by the indignation he felt when, as a teenager, he saw trash on Boca Chica beach, listened to fishermen describe the loss of catches, and participated in cleanups that, despite the effort, made it clear the solutions had to be structural. His coexistence with the territory allowed him to transform his feelings into actions that helped him take responsibility for the coastline he loved.

Together with his peers, he founded Upon the Waves with the purpose of getting Dominican youth involved in marine protection. The organisation has conducted coastal cleanups and educational workshops, forged strategic alliances, and promoted communication campaigns that translate scientific data into messages communities can understand and apply. Sánchez sees his role as both technical and facilitative. “We don’t just collect trash; we conduct data collection, record affected species, and document sources of pollution,” he stated, noting that technical evidence enabled the transition from beach activism to political advocacy.

Calling his activism “a way of life,” he emphasised that to be effective, you must combine community action, education, and technical work. He sometimes feels a sense of frustration, seeing the waste and wondering “whether we [are] doing enough,” but adds: “Not everything can be struggle. You also have to enjoy it.” Learning to delegate and build teams was crucial, and he has institutionalised the organisation’s processes.

Sánchez participated in data collection on the impact of styrofoam on marine ecosystems, waste registration, source-point analysis and sampling; the evidence base was included in an initial technical draft presented to the Ministry of Environment and Natural Resources, and ultimately informed the decision to restrict the use of styrofoam products in the country. In 2025–2026, authorities established phase-out deadlines and certification requirements for polystyrene-based products, marking a major step in the legislative and regulatory effort to curb their use and the resulting public policy regarding single-use plastics.

His advice for other young people who want to get involved? Learn to measure the scope of your commitment; seek technical training that allows you to work with evidence, not just impulses, and build community as opposed to being a solitary hero. For activists to preserve their mental and emotional well-being, he insisted that enjoying the environment provides an outlet to recharge in order to sustain the struggle in the medium and long term.

Evaristo Jiménez: Learning not to give up

At just 15 years old, Jiménez began a journey that took him from neighbourhood activities in La Romana to international forums. Five years later, he has not sought prominence, but meaning. “To me, an activist is anyone who acts with purpose,” he explained. “I believe what I do has purpose, so I 100 percent consider myself an activist.”

He began by joining youth projects in his province, where he learned about waste management issues, artisanal fishing, and the loss of coastal habitats. It was in that local space, working with initiatives such as Romana Joven, that he learned to organise educational campaigns, coordinate cleanup activities, and drive awareness initiatives in vulnerable neighbourhoods.

His continued collaboration with local organisations allowed him to professionalise his activism. He has done work with SOA Dominican Republic, Upon the Waves, and Generación por el Desarrollo, later joining projects with Fundación Blue Missions and collaborating on emerging Roots initiatives. “First it was local activism,” he recalled, “then national…and I’ve had the opportunity to reach international spaces.”

Each step involved assuming new obligations and technical learning; sometimes sacrifices had to be made. He turned down a scholarship at Universidad Católica Santo Domingo because there simply wasn't enough time to do both things: “I knew that if I accepted it, I would have to leave behind what I was building.” To combat the

anxiety and exhaustion he experienced in the midst of intense project coordination, he began to follow a structured schedule, delegated tasks, sought institutional allies, and reserved time for rest. At times when he felt inadequate to the task, he found solace in community, which allowed him to sustain action without burning out. “Passion does not exempt anyone from exhaustion,” he advised. “I found that it wasn’t about doing everything alone, but about building with others so the cause can survive.”

Rosángela Araujo: Building boundaries

At the intersection of youth, climate urgency, and social commitment, Rosángela Araujo’s voice stands out for its calmness and determination. Based in Santo Domingo, she became acutely aware of the fragility of natural spaces through time spent in nature as a child.

Her work with Buceo Ecológico RD and Parley for the Oceans may be more high-profile, but she also maintains constant participation in community festivals, educational campaigns, and environmental training projects. Seeing herself as “someone who helps improve and advance projects that already exist,” she prioritised strengthening existing collective structures instead of going the route of founding her own organisation.

Linking her identity as an activist to everyday coherence, Araujo strives to live according to the values she defends. Her work is not limited to participating in campaigns; rather, it involves a constant ethical commitment that tries to ensure daily decisions do not contradict what she preaches. “We all need to depend on a community,” she advised. “Don’t try to work alone.”

Early on, she explained, she developed an extreme relationship with environmental coherence. In her attempt not to pollute, she avoided any product containing plastic, even if it negatively impacted her well-being. She recalled periods in which she “spent days without properly drinking or eating” because she refused to buy packaged food, even when no healthy alternatives were available. Her behaviour was driven by a deep sense of environmental guilt and the idea that she “couldn’t fail the cause.”

The pressure resulted in exhaustion and temporary withdrawal from important projects whenever she felt overwhelmed. There were even times when she had to step away to avoid collapsing. “You’re going to have moments,” she said, “when you feel like you’re not enough…that nothing is going to change.” However, she learned to reinterpret those emotions and dismantle that internal narrative, calling it “a lie,” because “what we do does have an impact.” For her, every small change matters: when a family stops using foam, when a festival manages to raise awareness, when someone decides to consume more responsibly. Those actions, she explained, function as emotional anchors amid exhaustion.

One of the most important lessons in her process was accepting the need for professional support. “Sometimes we try to cover the sun with one finger…but yes, the climate crisis can lead you to a psychologist,” she stated. To cope with the most difficult moments, she incorporated systematic self-care practices into her routine: periodic trips outside the city, direct contact with nature, playful group dynamics, and spaces for honest conversations with peers. “I try to leave at least once every 15 days to reconnect with nature,” she shared.

Admitting that, at first, she spoke about oceans without having had direct experiences in them, Araujo emphasised the importance of deeply understanding what one defends. Over time, she learned to dive and snorkel, which strengthened her emotional connection with the sea and transformed her way of communicating — she no longer spoke only from data, but from lived experience. This allowed her to communicate urgent environmental messages with greater honesty and sensitivity.

Regarding the balance between personal life and activism, Araujo finally came to understand that not everything had to be solved immediately, and that the sustainability of the work largely depended on protecting her physical and emotional health: “If I break down, I can’t help anyone either.” Her advice for young people interested in environmental activism included normalising psychological support and not romanticising extreme sacrifice or self-exploitation.

Psychologist Eddy Frank Vásquez Sánchez, who is also an activist, noted that the shared experience of these three young people reveals a common pattern: episodes of extreme exhaustion, guilt over resting, a persistent sense of inadequacy, pressure to remain coherent all the time, and personal sacrifices that left emotional scars.

However, it also reveals a collective transformation, once they learned that caring for their mental health does not mean abandoning the struggle. Instead, it strengthens it from within. Resilience is built by weaving real support networks, asking for help without guilt, establishing boundaries, celebrating progress, accepting imperfection and knowing, above all, that it is possible to be an example, raise the Dominican flag in international spaces, and make your country proud in the fight for the preservation of its natural resources while still taking care of themselves — or, as Araujo puts it, “Joy is also resistance.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights significant environmental activism by young people in the Dominican Republic addressing urgent ecological challenges caused by tourism growth and waste management issues. It details concrete actions, legislative progress, and personal resilience strategies, showing broad societal impact and inspiring community-driven climate solutions.
articles.personality_title: Young activists lead the fight to protect the Dominican Republic’s coasts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
The Dominican Republic has seen a big rise in tourism, with nearly 12 million visitors in 2025. This growth brings money and jobs but also puts pressure on the environment, especially the country's long coastlines, rivers, and coral reefs. Waste is a major problem, with millions of tons of trash produced each year and many open dumps leaking pollution into nature.

**What happened**
Three young activists—Ismael Sánchez, Evaristo Jiménez, and Rosángela Araujo—are working hard to protect the environment in the Dominican Republic. They organize beach cleanups, run education programs, collect scientific data, and push for better laws. Their efforts helped lead to new rules to reduce harmful plastics like styrofoam. They also share how they handle the emotional challenges of activism, including stress and exhaustion, by building strong community support and taking care of their mental health.

**Impact**
These young leaders show that protecting nature needs both strong actions and personal balance. Their work has influenced government policies, helped raise public awareness, and inspired more people to join environmental efforts. They prove that even in a country facing big environmental problems, local youth can make a real difference by combining science, community work, and self-care.

**What's next step**
The activists plan to keep growing their organizations, strengthen partnerships, and push for better waste management and ocean protection laws. They want to train more young people to use data and science in activism and encourage others to care for their mental health while fighting for the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway**
Young people in the Dominican Republic are leading powerful, science-based environmental activism that balances community action with mental health care to protect their country’s coasts.
articles.personality_title_fr: De jeunes activistes protègent les côtes de la République dominicaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
La République dominicaine a connu une forte augmentation du tourisme, avec près de 12 millions de visiteurs en 2025. Cette croissance apporte de l'argent et des emplois, mais elle exerce aussi une pression sur l'environnement, notamment sur les côtes, les rivières et les récifs coralliens. Les déchets posent un problème majeur, avec des millions de tonnes de déchets produits chaque année et de nombreuses décharges à ciel ouvert qui polluent la nature.

**Ce qui s'est passé**
Trois jeunes activistes — Ismael Sánchez, Evaristo Jiménez et Rosángela Araujo — travaillent dur pour protéger l'environnement en République dominicaine. Ils organisent des nettoyages de plages, des programmes d'éducation, collectent des données scientifiques et poussent à de meilleures lois. Leurs actions ont contribué à l'adoption de nouvelles règles pour réduire les plastiques nocifs comme le polystyrène. Ils expliquent aussi comment ils gèrent les défis émotionnels de l'activisme, notamment le stress et l'épuisement, grâce à un fort soutien communautaire et à la prise en charge de leur santé mentale.

**Impact**
Ces jeunes leaders montrent que protéger la nature demande à la fois des actions fortes et un équilibre personnel. Leur travail a influencé les politiques gouvernementales, aidé à sensibiliser le public et inspiré plus de personnes à s'engager pour l'environnement. Ils prouvent que même dans un pays confronté à de grands problèmes environnementaux, les jeunes locaux peuvent faire une vraie différence en combinant science, travail communautaire et soin de soi.

**Prochaine étape**
Les activistes prévoient de continuer à développer leurs organisations, renforcer leurs partenariats et pousser pour une meilleure gestion des déchets et protection des océans. Ils veulent former davantage de jeunes à utiliser les données et la science dans l'activisme et encourager les autres à prendre soin de leur santé mentale tout en luttant pour l'environnement.

**Conclusion en une phrase**
Des jeunes de la République dominicaine mènent un activisme environnemental puissant et basé sur la science, qui allie action communautaire et soin de la santé mentale pour protéger leurs côtes.
articles.personality_title_es: Jóvenes activistas lideran la protección de las costas en República Dominicana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
La República Dominicana ha visto un gran aumento en el turismo, con casi 12 millones de visitantes en 2025. Este crecimiento trae dinero y empleos, pero también genera presión sobre el medio ambiente, especialmente en las costas, ríos y arrecifes de coral. Los desechos son un problema importante, con millones de toneladas de basura al año y muchos vertederos abiertos que contaminan la naturaleza.

**Qué pasó**
Tres jóvenes activistas — Ismael Sánchez, Evaristo Jiménez y Rosángela Araujo — están trabajando para proteger el medio ambiente en la República Dominicana. Organizan limpiezas de playas, programas educativos, recogen datos científicos y promueven mejores leyes. Sus esfuerzos ayudaron a crear nuevas reglas para reducir plásticos dañinos como el poliestireno. También comparten cómo manejan el estrés y agotamiento del activismo, apoyándose en la comunidad y cuidando su salud mental.

**Impacto**
Estos jóvenes líderes muestran que proteger la naturaleza requiere acciones fuertes y equilibrio personal. Su trabajo ha influido en políticas del gobierno, aumentado la conciencia pública e inspirado a más personas a unirse a la causa ambiental. Demuestran que, aun con grandes problemas, los jóvenes locales pueden lograr cambios reales combinando ciencia, trabajo comunitario y autocuidado.

**Próximo paso**
Los activistas planean seguir creciendo sus organizaciones, fortalecer alianzas y promover mejores leyes para gestionar los desechos y proteger los océanos. Quieren formar a más jóvenes en el uso de datos y ciencia en el activismo y fomentar el cuidado de la salud mental mientras luchan por el medio ambiente.

**Conclusión en una frase**
Jóvenes en República Dominicana lideran un activismo ambiental fuerte y científico que combina acción comunitaria y cuidado de la salud mental para proteger sus costas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of three intertwined coral reefs shaped like youthful human silhouettes rising from Dominican Republic’s turquoise coastline, surrounded by gentle waves carrying small clusters of plastic debris being lifted away by vibrant schools of fish, all rendered in soft natural tones to symbolize young ecological activists nurturing and protecting their marine environment with resilience and hope.

articles.title: The last-ditch plan to save coral reefs from utter destruction
articles.summary: Bleaching has devastated reefs around the world, raising fears of an irreversible shift. Yet new interventions have revealed that corals can be remarkably resilient if we can give them enough help to recover
articles.content: Bleaching has devastated reefs around the world, raising fears of an irreversible shift. Yet new interventions have revealed that corals can be remarkably resilient if we can give them enough help to recover
articles.reasoning: The article discusses new interventions that help coral reefs recover from bleaching, indicating a positive environmental impact with broad significance for marine ecosystems and biodiversity.
articles.personality_title: New efforts show coral reefs can recover from bleaching
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Coral reefs around the world have suffered severe damage due to bleaching, a process where corals lose their color and health because of rising ocean temperatures.

**What happened** – Scientists have started using new methods to help coral reefs recover. These techniques support the corals’ natural ability to bounce back after bleaching events.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows that coral reefs are not doomed to disappear. With the right help, they can survive and continue to support many sea creatures that depend on them.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to improve these recovery methods and work on applying them to more coral reefs worldwide to protect marine life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New ways to help coral reefs recover offer hope for saving these vital underwater ecosystems from destruction.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles méthodes montrent que les récifs coralliens peuvent se remettre du blanchissement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les récifs coralliens du monde entier ont été gravement endommagés par le blanchissement, un phénomène où les coraux perdent leur couleur et leur santé à cause de la hausse des températures océaniques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont commencé à utiliser de nouvelles méthodes pour aider les récifs coralliens à se remettre. Ces techniques soutiennent la capacité naturelle des coraux à se rétablir après des épisodes de blanchissement.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre que les récifs coralliens ne sont pas condamnés à disparaître. Avec la bonne aide, ils peuvent survivre et continuer à soutenir de nombreuses espèces marines qui en dépendent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer ces méthodes de récupération et à les appliquer à plus de récifs coralliens dans le monde pour protéger la vie marine.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – De nouvelles façons d’aider les récifs coralliens à se rétablir offrent un espoir pour sauver ces écosystèmes sous-marins essentiels de la destruction.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas técnicas muestran que los arrecifes de coral pueden recuperarse del blanqueo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los arrecifes de coral en todo el mundo han sufrido daños graves por el blanqueo, un proceso donde los corales pierden su color y salud debido al aumento de la temperatura del océano.

**Qué pasó** – Los científicos han comenzado a usar nuevos métodos para ayudar a que los arrecifes de coral se recuperen. Estas técnicas apoyan la capacidad natural de los corales para recuperarse después de eventos de blanqueo.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que los arrecifes de coral no están condenados a desaparecer. Con la ayuda adecuada, pueden sobrevivir y seguir apoyando a muchas criaturas marinas que dependen de ellos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando estos métodos de recuperación y trabajarán para aplicarlos a más arrecifes de coral en todo el mundo para proteger la vida marina.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Nuevas formas de ayudar a los arrecifes de coral a recuperarse ofrecen esperanza para salvar estos ecosistemas submarinos vitales de la destrucción.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant underwater scene of a coral reef showing colorful, healthy corals gradually regrowing and glowing softly amidst patches of bleached white coral, with gentle rays of warm sunlight filtering through clear water, symbolizing hope and resilience.

articles.title: Dinosaur-killing asteroid impact site stayed hot for millions of years
articles.summary: Drill cores at the impact site of the Chicxulub asteroid show evidence that, alongside widespread destruction, the collision created a vast underground ecosystem filled with hot water that sheltered microbial life
articles.content:
Drill cores at the impact site of the Chicxulub asteroid show evidence that, alongside widespread destruction, the collision created a vast underground ecosystem filled with hot water that sheltered microbial life

Illustration of the Chicxulub asteroid impact, which took place 66 million years ago MARK GARLICK/Science Photo Library/Getty Images

The asteroid strike that wiped out the dinosaurs hit with such force that it took at least 8 million years for the impact site to cool down, creating a warm underground ecosystem where microscopic life thrived.

The Chicxulub asteroid, which collided with Earth 66 million years ago at what is now Mexico, is thought to have been as large as 15 kilometres in diameter. The strike caused so much climate chaos that it wiped out three-quarters of species on Earth. All the dinosaurs except the ancestors of birds became extinct and a nuclear winter gripped the planet for at least 15 years.

Advertisement

Its effects were also felt deep underground. “The Chicxulub impact was big enough to cause deformation at least 35 kilometres under the surface of the Earth, detectable using geophysical surveys,” says Annemarie Pickersgill at the University of Glasgow, UK.

The impact melted about 10,000 cubic kilometres of rock, she says. The combination of melted rock and seawater created porous material filled with tiny pockets of hot water, known as a hydrothermal system.

Because of the presence of minerals that only form where there is liquid water and heat, we know that the asteroid would have created hydrothermal environments to depths of several kilometres. But the scale and lifespan of the heating and resulting hydrothermal system has, it seems, been massively underestimated.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

Previously, it was thought it took only 2 million years for the impact site to cool down. Now, Pickersgill and her colleagues say it may have taken at least four times longer, giving hydrothermal life much more time to thrive.

“One of the biggest unknowns about all impact-generated hydrothermal systems, and Chicxulub in particular, is how long the heat keeps water circulating through the structure,” says Pickersgill.

To figure this out, the team drilled 1 kilometre into the crater to obtain rock cores. Because potassium in the rocks has decayed into argon gas over time, the researchers could measure the amount of argon trapped in the samples to find out their age.

“We got a range of ages from the time of impact at 66 million years ago to about 58 million years ago,” says Pickersgill. “That told us that hydrothermal activity was ongoing in at least part of the Chicxulub structure for 8 million years after the impact.”

Sulphur isotopes in the cores provide evidence that microbial life existed in the hydrothermal system and recovered rapidly after the impact.

The results mean that the very earliest impact craters on the young Earth – and perhaps other worlds – may also have had habitable hydrothermal systems for longer than previously known.

“This provides more opportunity for life to develop, evolve and spread,” says Pickersgill. “It supports the concept that early life on Earth may have found a long-term home in impact craters, and possibly even life on other planets where these massive impact craters are dominant surface features.”

Chris Kirkland at Curtin University in Perth, Australia, says while there is “not an entirely unambiguous record of continuous hydrothermal activity” at Chicxulub, the evidence is strong that the impact site stayed hot for millions of years.

“Large impacts do not simply destroy environments,” he says. “They can also create long-lived underground systems where hot fluids circulate through shattered rock. These chemically rich settings may provide sheltered habitats for microbes and perhaps even favourable conditions for some of the early chemical steps towards life.”
articles.reasoning: The article reveals a significant scientific discovery about the Chicxulub asteroid impact site sustaining a long-lived hydrothermal system that supported microbial life for millions of years. This finding broadens understanding of early life habitats on Earth and potentially other planets, highlighting a positive impact on knowledge of life’s resilience and planetary habitability.
articles.personality_title: Asteroid impact site stayed hot for 8 million years, supporting underground life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – About 66 million years ago, a huge asteroid hit Earth near what is now Mexico. This event, called the Chicxulub impact, caused massive destruction and ended the age of dinosaurs.

**What happened** – Scientists drilled deep into the impact crater and studied rock samples. They found that the site stayed hot for at least 8 million years after the impact. This heat created underground pockets filled with hot water, forming a special environment where tiny life forms called microbes could live.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that the asteroid’s impact did more than just cause destruction. It also created warm underground spaces that sheltered life for millions of years. This helps us understand how life might have survived and even started in extreme places, both on Earth and possibly on other planets.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will likely study other ancient impact sites to see if they also had long-lasting warm environments. This could help scientists learn more about how life began and spread on Earth and guide the search for life beyond our planet.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Chicxulub asteroid impact created a long-lasting warm underground habitat that supported microbial life for millions of years after the dinosaurs died out.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le site de l’impact de l’astéroïde est resté chaud pendant 8 millions d’années, soutenant la vie souterraine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a environ 66 millions d’années, un énorme astéroïde a frappé la Terre près de l’actuel Mexique. Cet événement, appelé impact de Chicxulub, a causé une destruction massive et mis fin à l’ère des dinosaures.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont foré profondément dans le cratère d’impact et étudié des échantillons de roche. Ils ont découvert que le site est resté chaud pendant au moins 8 millions d’années après l’impact. Cette chaleur a créé des poches souterraines remplies d’eau chaude, formant un environnement spécial où de minuscules formes de vie appelées microbes pouvaient vivre.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que l’impact de l’astéroïde a fait plus que détruire. Il a aussi créé des espaces souterrains chauds qui ont protégé la vie pendant des millions d’années. Cela aide à comprendre comment la vie a pu survivre et même commencer dans des endroits extrêmes, sur Terre et peut-être sur d’autres planètes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement étudier d’autres anciens sites d’impact pour voir s’ils avaient aussi des environnements chauds durables. Cela pourrait aider à mieux comprendre comment la vie a commencé et s’est répandue sur Terre, et guider la recherche de la vie ailleurs dans l’espace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’impact de l’astéroïde Chicxulub a créé un habitat souterrain chaud et durable qui a soutenu la vie microbienne pendant des millions d’années après la disparition des dinosaures.
articles.personality_title_es: El sitio del impacto del asteroide permaneció caliente durante 8 millones de años, apoyando vida subterránea
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace unos 66 millones de años, un enorme asteroide chocó contra la Tierra cerca de lo que hoy es México. Este evento, llamado impacto de Chicxulub, causó una gran destrucción y puso fin a la era de los dinosaurios.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos perforaron profundamente en el cráter del impacto y estudiaron muestras de roca. Descubrieron que el sitio permaneció caliente durante al menos 8 millones de años después del impacto. Este calor creó espacios subterráneos llenos de agua caliente, formando un ambiente especial donde pequeños seres vivos llamados microbios podían vivir.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que el impacto del asteroide hizo más que destruir. También creó espacios cálidos bajo tierra que protegieron la vida durante millones de años. Esto ayuda a entender cómo la vida pudo sobrevivir e incluso comenzar en lugares extremos, tanto en la Tierra como posiblemente en otros planetas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente estudiarán otros sitios antiguos de impactos para ver si también tuvieron ambientes cálidos duraderos. Esto podría ayudar a aprender más sobre cómo comenzó y se extendió la vida en la Tierra y guiar la búsqueda de vida en otros lugares.

**Resumen en una frase** – El impacto del asteroide Chicxulub creó un hábitat subterráneo cálido y duradero que apoyó la vida microbiana durante millones de años después de la extinción de los dinosaurios.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast underground hydrothermal cavern beneath a shattered ancient Earth landscape, glowing softly with pockets of hot water and mineral formations, where tiny abstract microbial shapes thrive amidst fractured rocks, illuminated by gentle amber and ochre tones that evoke enduring heat and life’s resilience after a massive asteroid impact.
